data_source: Berkshire Hathaway 2017 annual report and 2014 special letter

g4_revenue_trend.description: Buffett later disclosed that premium volume at National Indemnity and its sister operations was $22M in 1967 and grew dramatically over time without new Berkshire shares being issued.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: The attraction was disciplined underwriting, not aggressive premium growth. Buffett described the business as one that usually delivered an underwriting profit.

g7_moat.description: National Indemnity's moat was disciplined underwriting culture plus the structural advantage of insurance float. Buffett was effectively paying a modest premium over tangible net worth for a business that usually earned an underwriting profit while delivering investable float equal to almost three times that net worth. That combination created a financing advantage most industrial businesses simply do not have.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — large insurer, but without the same owner-operator purchase economics or uniquely attractive float-to-price relationship.
PASS — a respectable insurer, but not available on anything like National Indemnity's purchase terms.

buffett_action: Berkshire bought National Indemnity and a smaller sister company for $8.6M in early 1967.
actual_result: The foundational Berkshire acquisition. Insurance float became the engine that financed decades of compounding.
key_insight: Buffett was not just buying earnings; he was buying a conservative underwriting machine attached to permanently reusable capital.
